preFix.title: Building a small pond for surface water management
preFix.subtitle: A flexible-liner wildlife pond that also slows and infiltrates roof and driveway runoff
preFix.excerpt: A 2 to 4 m² flexible-liner pond at the lowest point of a garden can absorb roof water from one downpipe, reduce fast runoff from impermeable surfaces, and create a wildlife habitat. This is the site selection, excavation, liner installation, and overflow design for a combined wildlife and surface-water management pond.
preFix.sourceNotes: CIRIA SuDS Manual (C753, 2015) on ponds and swales for surface water management. Royal Horticultural Society 'Wildlife pond' guidance. The Wildlife Trusts pond creation factsheets. DEFRA 'Sustainable drainage: non-statutory technical standards' (2015).

content.text: A small garden pond creates habitat for frogs, newts, insects. And birds, and stores heavy rainfall that would otherwise run off into the drain. A 2 m² pond 600 mm deep holds 1.2 m³ of water. Enough to absorb runoff from around 25 to 30 m² of roof in a moderate rain event. With an overflow route to a soakaway or lawn, it becomes part of a sustainable drainage network.
Choose the lowest point in the garden, downhill from the downpipe or driveway being managed.
Minimum 3 m from any building foundation.
Avoid locations directly under deciduous trees: leaf fall adds nutrients and promotes algae.
Partial shade is fine. Full sun promotes evaporation and algae. Full shade reduces wildlife value.
Mark the pond outline on the ground. A kidney or irregular shape fits better into a garden edge than a rectangle and creates more marginal planting opportunities.

Excavate in zones: a shallow marginal shelf 200 mm deep around part of the perimeter. A mid-zone at 400 mm. And a deep zone at 600 to 700 mm. The graduated depth reduces algae by creating different temperature zones.
Calculate liner size: length = maximum pond length plus twice the maximum depth plus 500 mm overhang. Same calculation for width. For a 2.5 x 2 m pond, 700 mm deep: liner = x = 4.4 x 3.9 m.
Install 50 mm of sharp sand or fibrous underlay over the entire excavated surface before the liner. Remove any protruding stones.
Drape the flexible liner over the excavation. Add water weight gradually to pull the liner into shape. Fold pleats at the corners as neatly as you can rather than fighting the wrinkles.
Trim the liner leaving 300 mm overlap at the edge. Peg the edge down with large flat stones or turf.

Route a downpipe via a first-flush diverter into the pond at the shallow marginal end. Place a stone diffuser under the inlet to slow the incoming flow and prevent scouring the liner. Route the overflow notch or standpipe to a lawn area, a soakaway. Or a secondary water butt 150 mm lower in the garden. The overflow must be lower than the liner edge to prevent the liner lifting during a storm.

attrs.symptom: Pond level drops by 25 mm per week in summer

content.text: This is normal evaporation in warm weather: a 2 m² pond loses 50 litres per week at peak summer. Top up with water butt water, not tap water, to avoid chlorine affecting aquatic life.

attrs.symptom: Green water algae within weeks of filling

content.text: Normal in a new pond. Add submerged oxygenating plants which outcompete algae for nutrients. Avoid fertilisers near the pond. Green water usually clears within 4 to 6 weeks once plants establish.

initialFindings.message: paragraph reads at grade 11.3 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
initialFindings.snippet: Avoid locations directly under deciduous trees: leaf fall adds nutrients and promotes algae.
